verses.text: God's first words to Abram are three losses — leave your country, your relatives, your father's house. Each circle of belonging is named. The call goes from the outermost ring to the most painful.

Three gifts follow the call: I will make of thee a great nation, I will bless thee, I will make thy name great. The chapter just ended judged people trying to make their own name. Now God offers Abram a name as a gift.
And then the line the whole Bible turns on: in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed. Abram is being chosen, but not for his own sake. He is chosen so that the world might be blessed.
Abram does what God says. He is seventy-five — old enough that uprooting is not an adventure but a sacrifice. Obedience here looks like quiet, costly trust.
He brings Sarai, his nephew Lot, and everything they own — including the souls they had gathered in Haran. The household is moving as one, into a land they have never seen.

Abram passes through the land all the way to Shechem. There the LORD appears to him with a new promise: this land — the land currently full of Canaanites — will belong to your offspring. Abram builds his first altar.

Abram passes through the land all the way to Shechem. There the LORD appears to him with a new promise: this land — the land currently full of Canaanites — will belong to your offspring. Abram builds his first altar.
Abram moves on to a hill between Bethel and Hai. He builds another altar and calls upon the name of the LORD. Worship marks his footsteps across the land.
Abram keeps moving south, deeper into the land. His journey through Canaan is not a settlement — it is a pilgrim's walk through the future inheritance.
A famine hits, and Abram heads down to Egypt for survival. The man God promised would inherit Canaan is already leaving it.
Fearing for his life because Sarai is beautiful, Abram asks her to say she is his sister, not his wife. The man God just called is already managing his own safety with a half-truth.
Fearing for his life because Sarai is beautiful, Abram asks her to say she is his sister, not his wife. The man God just called is already managing his own safety with a half-truth.
Fearing for his life because Sarai is beautiful, Abram asks her to say she is his sister, not his wife. The man God just called is already managing his own safety with a half-truth.
Pharaoh's men see Sarai's beauty, take her into Pharaoh's house, and shower Abram with sheep, oxen, camels, and servants. Abram grows wealthy through a deception that cost his wife dearly.
Pharaoh's men see Sarai's beauty, take her into Pharaoh's house, and shower Abram with sheep, oxen, camels, and servants. Abram grows wealthy through a deception that cost his wife dearly.
Pharaoh's men see Sarai's beauty, take her into Pharaoh's house, and shower Abram with sheep, oxen, camels, and servants. Abram grows wealthy through a deception that cost his wife dearly.
God strikes Pharaoh and his household with plagues — protecting Sarai and the promise, even when Abram failed to protect them himself. The covenant doesn't depend on Abram's perfection.
Pharaoh confronts Abram: Why did you do this? What is this you have done unto me? The pagan king lectures the man of God on honesty. Sometimes outsiders see our compromises more clearly than we do.
Pharaoh confronts Abram: Why did you do this? What is this you have done unto me? The pagan king lectures the man of God on honesty. Sometimes outsiders see our compromises more clearly than we do.
Pharaoh sends Abram out with everything he had. He returns to Canaan — and to the lessons not yet learned.
